coredata.dc:title: A Green and lower-temperature synthesis of two-color fluorescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Xue, Zhipeng
Gao, Hui
Li, Xiaolong
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were produced by a facile thermal treating strategy at low temperature using citric acid (CA) and urea as the precursors. The as-prepared N-GQDs with different ratios of the precursors exhibited blue or green emission with relatively high quantum yield in the ethanol solution. While in the aqueous solution, the quantum dots displayed both blue and green emissions, which indicate two independent luminescent centers coexisted in the N-GQDs. Considering of the excellent absorption ability of the N-GQDs in the visible-light region, the N-GQDs was combined with titanium dioxide (TiO2) as the dye sensitizer. And, the products showed 16 times catalytic degradation ability than that of pure TiO2 for rhodamine B (RhB).
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped graphene quantum dots
Two-color fluorescent emission
Dye sensitizer
Photocatalytize degradation

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image of N-GQDs; (b), (c) The corresponding size distribution and high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image of N-GQDs; (d) the Raman spectrum of N-GQDs.
(a) The XPS full survey of the 1:4 N-GQDs; The C 1s (b) and N 1s (c) high-resolution XPS spectra of the 1:4 N-GQDs; (d) The FT-IR spectra of the 1:4 N-GQDs.

The absorption spectra (a) and PL spectra under the 400 nm irradiation (b) of the N-GQDs with different ratios; (c) The PL and PLE spectra of 1:4 N-GQDs; (d) The chromaticity coordinate of 1:4 N-GQDs.
(a) The absorption spectrum of 1:4 N-GQDs aqueous solution; (b) The PLE spectrum of 1:4 N-GQDs aqueous solution; (c), (d) The PL spectrum under different wavelength emissions.
(a) The UCPL spectra of N-GQDs; (b) The proposed energy-level diagram of the N-GQDs.
The O 1s XPS spectra of the N-GQDs/TiO2 composites Solid 1 (a) and Solid 2 (b); (c), (d) Photocatalytic performance of TiO2, N-GQDs, Solid 1 and Solid 2 under visible light(λ > 400 nm).
The possible structure of the N-GQDs.
The possible photocatalystic mechanism of N-GQDs/TiO2 under the visible light.

head.ce:title: A Green and lower-temperature synthesis of two-color fluorescent nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Physical Science and Technology, Key Laboratory for Magnetism and Magnetic Materials of Ministry of Education, National & Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Optical Conversion Materials and Technology, Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design, Ministry of Education, Lanzhou University, Lanzhou 730000, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Physical Science and Technology
Key Laboratory for Magnetism and Magnetic Materials of Ministry of Education
National & Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Optical Conversion Materials and Technology
Key Laboratory of Special Function Materials and Structure Design

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Synchrotron Radiation Facility, Shanghai Institute of Applied Physics, Chines Academy of Sciences, Shanghai 201203, PR China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contribute equally to the article.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) were produced by a facile thermal treating strategy at low temperature using citric acid (CA) and urea as the precursors. The as-prepared N-GQDs with different ratios of the precursors exhibited blue or green emission with relatively high quantum yield in the ethanol solution. While in the aqueous solution, the quantum dots displayed both blue and green emissions, which indicate two independent luminescent centers coexisted in the N-GQDs. Considering of the excellent absorption ability of the N-GQDs in the visible-light region, the N-GQDs was combined with titanium dioxide (TiO2) as the dye sensitizer. And, the products showed 16 times catalytic degradation ability than that of pure TiO2 for rhodamine B (RhB).

ce:section.ce:para: TiO2 has a large-scale application in the photocatalyzed hydrogen evolution [1,2], environmental pollution treatment [3] and dye-sensitized solar cells [4]. However, TiO2 can only utilize the ultraviolet part of sunlight due to the wide band-gap of 3.2 eV [5]. For increasing utilization efficiency of the sunlight, one effective rout is coupling with other semiconductors or dye sensitizers which have the visible light absorption.1∼4

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs), as an interesting carbon nano-material, are graphene sheets of less than 100 nm in size and several layers in thickness [7]. Due to their excellent optical and electrical property, low-toxicity and non-pollution, this nano-material is promising to replace semi-conductor quantum dots (SQDs) in solar cells [8], light emitting devices [9], photocatalysts [6], supercapacitor [10], bioimaging [1] and drug delivery [11]. But the GQDs also exist some deficiencies like low quantum field and stability. To improve the photo-electric properties of the GQDs, one method is introducing other heteroatoms or functional groups to adjust the electronic densities of the GQDs [8,14]. In numerous doping elements, the nitrogen atom, with a comparable atomic size (atomic radius: C∼70 p.m. and N∼56 p.m.) [12] and five valence electrons to bond with carbon atom, has attracted many attentions. Fang et al. [13] applied N-GQDs as the detection of aluminum ions which the limit of detection was 1.3 μmol L−1. Kundu et al. [12] prepared the N, F, S-GQDs which have a quantum yield above 70%. Nowadays, more attentions were focus on the applications in the photoelectric conversion. For achieving high conversion rate, many researchers imported N-GQDs into coupling with TiO2 which can make better use of the excitation electrons from the photoluminescence (PL) process. Tetsuka et al. combined multiple N-containing groups doped GQDs, perovskite and TiO2 to produce optoelectronic devices with a photoelectric conversion rate of 6.77% [8]. Pang et al. combined N-GQDs and TiO2 as quantitative detector of pcDNA3-HBV with a detection limit was 0.005 fmol/L [14].

Herein, a facile isothermal one-pot synthesizing process was utilized into producing the N-GQDs. The as-obtained N-GQDs have different color emissions by heating the different ratios of two reactants (citric acid for GQDs and urea for nitrogen doping). The GQDs exhibit relatively high quantum yields (QYs) (16.7–38%) in the ethanol solution and showed two-color emissions in the aqueous solution (Fig.S3). And in order to studying the potential application of N-GQDs in organic dyes degradation, the composites combined with N-GQDs and P25 TiO2 were obtained. After analyzing and comparing these composites, it is found that the interaction between two components can cause a great increasing of the degradation property. Besides that, all of this samples illustrate an excellent photocatalytic performance which is about 10–16 times that of P25 TiO2 under the light with the wavelength larger than 400 nm. Therefore, the as-prepared GQDs are hopeful to be considered as the active component of efficient photocatalysts in multiple pollutants degradation.

The CA was purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent, the urea was purchased from Tianjin Kaixin Chemical, the P25 TiO2 was from Degussa. All chemicals were analytical grade and used without further purification.

In the procedure, 0.19 g (1 mmol) CA and 0.24 g (4 mmol) urea were dissolved in 5.0 mL of water. The solution was transferred to an oven and heated at 140 °C for 3 h. After naturally cooling down to room temperature, the solid product was rinsed with alcohol and the solution was dried at 50 °C to obtain brown powder, (for separating the N-GQDs more conveniently, ethanol was used as the solvent) which is the N-GQDs with the NCA:Nurea precursor ratio of 1:4 (1:4 N-GQDs). Also, the N-GQDs with others precursors ratios (including 1:1, 1:2, 1:3, 1:5, 1:6 and 1:7) were synthesized in this studying by the similar procedure.

1) 50 mg P25 TiO2 and 15 mg 1:4 N-GQDs were dispersed into the 30 ml water and kept stirring for 12 h, named Sample 1. Then the solution of Sample 1 was transferred into a 20 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was heated to 110 °C in an electric oven and kept there for an additional 2 h, Which named Sample 2.
The Sample 1 was centrifuged at 8500 rpm/min for 30 min, and the solid was dried at 70 °C, called Solid 1; Sample 2 was also processed using the same procedure, which resulting in Solid 2.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns are obtained on a Bruker D2 PHASER X-ray diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation source (λ = 1.54184 Å) to determine the crystal phase of the obtained samples. The Ramman spectrum is measured by Horiba Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution Raman spectrometer with a 473 nm wavelength laser. The morphology of the sample and the energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) spectra are detected by field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, Hitachi, 30 S-3400 N). The ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra are obtained by a UV-Vis spectrophotometer (LG-722SP). In addition, transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images are collected on an F30 S-TWIN electron microscope (Tecnai G2, FEI Company). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI-5702, Physical Electronics) is performed using a monochromated Al Kα irradiation. PL spectra are measured at room temperature using a FLS-920T fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with a 450 W Xe light source and double excitation monochromators. The infrared spectroscopy (IR) spectra are obtained by fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FT-IR, NEXUS 670, Nicolet Company) with KBr as reference. The time resolved photoluminescence decay measurement is done via fluorescence spectrophotometer(FLS920, Edinbergh company).

The photocatalytic activities of the N-GQDs/TiO2 composite, N-GQDs and TiO2 are measured by the decomposition of rhodamine B (RhB) solution (10 mg/L, 50 mL) under various light irradiation. In CEL-HXF300 photochemical reaction instrument, 10 mg photocatalysts are suspended in the RhB solution. Before irradiation, the suspensions are stirred in the dark for 60 min to ensure the establishment of adsorption–desorption equilibrium. Then, a 210 W Xe lamp equipped with a UV cutoff filter (λ = 400–800 nm) is irradiated to start the photocatalytic reaction. A series of certain volume of suspension are withdrawn at selected times for analysis, centrifuge suspension to obtain the supernatant fluid and the concentration of dye solution is analyzed by measuring the light absorption of the clear solution at 554 nm (λ max for RhB solution) using the UV-vis spectrophotometer (LG-722SP).

Fig. 1 includes the morphology and microstructure information of N-GQDs. Fig. 1a and b show that the average size is 2.24 nm. The representative HRTEM image (Fig. 1c) displays a lattice spacing distance of 3.25 Å, which is similar to that of graphite (002) plane [15], respectively. As shown in Fig. S1a and b, the surface morphology of the N-GQDs deposited on a silicon substrate was investigated by atomic force microscopy and reveals a height of around 1.4 nm, suggesting that the as-prepared N-GQDs are consist of about 4 monolayers of graphene [11]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the sample shows a broad diffraction peak at 2θ = 25° (see Fig. S1c) which corresponds to the (002) planes of graphite [16]. In Fig. 1d, the Raman spectra (excitation wavelength is 525 nm) of the N-GQDs reveals two prominent peaks at 1356.5 cm−1 and 1586.2 cm−1, corresponding to the documented D and G bands, respectively [17]. The intensity ratio of the D to G band (ID/IG) is a measurement of the disorder extent, as well as the ratio of sp3/sp2 carbons. In this case, the ID/IG ratio of the samples is around 0.83, which is close to that of the graphene oxide [18] and means the relatively high-quality GQDs being produced. To sum it all up, the above analyses indicate that the as-prepared samples are highly crystalline and graphitic materials.

For the further information, XPS and FT-IR spectroscopy analyses were carried out to determine the as-prepared N-GQDs. Fig. 2 a presents three peaks in the full survey spectrum of the N-GQDs, which correspond to carbon (284.5 eV), nitrogen (399.5 eV) and oxygen (531.5 eV). The spectrum of C 1s (Fig. 2b) includes three prominent peaks which appears at 284.8, 285.9 and 288.4 eV, corresponding to sp2 C in graphene (C-C), sp3 C in C=N and O-C=O [19,20]. And in Fig. 2c, there are two pronounced peaks at 399.8 eV and 400.9 eV, which can be attributed to the pyrrolic N (C-N-C) and graphitic N/N-H bands, respectively. For getting more elements details about these N-GQDs with different NCA:Nurea ratios, a series of XPS analyses about these quantum dots (QDs) were desired (in Fig. S2a). In the C 1s spectrum of 1:2 N-GQDs, the middle peak corresponds to the C-O bond. With the urea addition increasing, the middle peak shifts to a lower binding energy, which represents C=N bonds. It is clearly that there are more C=N bonds when more urea in the synthesis system. Fig. 2d is the FT-IR spectrum of 1:4 N-GQD. For the as prepared sample, the 3430 cm−1 peak corresponds to O-H stretching vibration. The 1714 cm−1 and 1070 cm−1 peak resulted from the stretching vibrations from C=O and C-O. The nitrogen-containing groups appeared at 3215 cm−1 (N-H in-plane stretching), 1615 cm−1 (-CO-NH-), 1340 cm−1 and 1420 cm−1 (C-N stretching vibrations and in-plane of amide-carbonyl). And in Fig. S2b, the FT-IR spectra of others ratios N-GQDs (including 1:2, 1:4, 1:7) also have the similar characteristic peaks (although there are some distinctions of detail in the spectra of these different N-GQDs). By all above results, a possible structure of the N-GQDs has been speculated in Scheme 1 .

In Fig. 3 a, a series of UV-vis absorption spectra of the N-GQDs with different ratios of NCA: Nurea display the continual changing of the absorption peaks. There are two prominent absorption peaks (∼350 nm and ∼400 nm) attributed to the n→π* transitions of C=O and C=N [21], and a shoulder peak at 270 nm due to the peptides (-CO-NH-) connecting with benzene [22] in these spectra. Increasing the value of Nurea, the absorption peaks ratio of C=N to C=O (AC=N/AC=O) also increases. Besides, the EDX analyses of the series of N-GQDs with different NCA:Nurea ratios, as shown in Table. S1, indicate that N concentration of the samples increases with the higher urea addition. In summary of Fig. 2, Table. S1 and Fig. S2, the N-GQDs with higher N percentage could be obtained by adding more urea into the synthesis system; Meanwhile, with the increasing nitrogen content of N-GQDs, the absorption spectrum is dominated by the transition of C=N. Moreover, the PL spectra (Fig. 3b) of these N-GQDs exhibit a trend that the emission shift from blue to green under the same excitation wavelength of 400 nm (the wavelength displacement is 51–55 nm).

In addition to the PL emission displacement, these N-GQDs also have different quantum yields, including 1:1 (16.7%), 1:2 (18.6%), 1:3 (37%), 1:4 (38.3%), 1:5 (27%), 1:6(29.3%), 1:7 (24.6%). It is clearly that the 1:4 N-GQDs have the highest transfer efficiency. In Fig. 3c, the PL and PLE spectra of the 1:4 N-GQDs were studied by using different excitation wavelengths. In the spectra, the N-GQDs show the excitation-dependent PL behavior: the PL emission peaks shift from 496 nm to 539 nm with the excitation wavelength (λex) being changed from 340 nm to 450 nm. As a whole, the intensity of the peaks increases firstly and then decreases with excitation wavelength increasing, and the strongest emission peak is located at 523 nm with the excitation of 412 nm. Additional, the spectra of 1:4, 1:5, 1:6 and 1:7 N-GQDs have the approximate strongest emission peaks under the excitation of 400 nm (in Fig. 3b). These results indicate a relationship: with the synthesis system including more N resource (urea), the products contain more N-groups (C=N) and less O-containing groups (O-groups). Due to this changing, the emission of the GQDs shifts to the green light which corresponds to the emission of C=N. On the other side, the lower wavelength emissions of the 1:1 and 1:2 N-GQDs are on account of the high oxygen and O-groups content.

An interesting thing is, as shown in Fig. S3b, when dissolved into water, the 1:4 N-GQDs exhibit two independent PL regions with emission peaks at 459 nm and 535 nm (Fig. 4 c and d), which indicating that two chromophores are excited by the light of 365 nm. Moreover, the PL excitation (PLE) spectrum in Fig. 4b also confirms it. This phenomenon is similar to the GQDs prepared by a hydrothermal graphene oxide reduction process [23] and the S,N co-doped GQDs synthesized by solvothermal route [1], meanwhile contrary to the PL spectrum of as-prepared N-GQDs ethanol solution (Fig. 3c). From Fig. 4a, it can be found that a separation of the broad absorption peak into two single peaks due to the solvent being changed from ethanol to water. The two peaks are attributed to the n→π* transitions of C=O and C=N. So, to explain the phenomena, a possibility changing is provided. In aqueous solution, -CO-NH- groups are hydrolyzed partly into carboxyl (-COOH) and primary amine (-NH2), and this process is confirmed by the pH of the N-GQDs aqueous solution less than 7 (Fig. S5, pH≈6) and the absorption peaks ratio of peptide to C=N (Apeptides/AC=N) changing from 0.664 (in alcohol) to 0.707 (in water).
(1) R -NH-CO-R’+H2O ⇌ R-NH2+ R′-COOH

Then, the primary amine can synthesize imine group (C=N) with aldehyde (-CHO) or ketone (-CO-) and lead to a longer wavelength emission peak.
R-NH2 + R′-CHO ⇌ R′-CH=N-R + H2O
or
(2) R-NH2 + R′-CO-R’’ ⇌ R″-CR’ = N-R + H2O

On the other hand, more imines being synthesized causes the concentration of C=O decreasing. The evidence is that the AC=N/AC=O increased from 1.02 to 1.10 with solvent from ethanol to water. Besides, due to the carboxyl connected with graphene increasing, a single peak at 338 nm appears in absorption spectrum (Fig. 4a), and this absorption causes a single blue emission just as shown in Fig. 4c.

In addition, the as-prepared N-GQDs have the up-conversion phenomenon. The up-converted PL (UCPL) spectrum of 1:4 N-GQDs (ethanol solution) is illustrated in Fig. 5 a. The excitation wavelength is shifted from 650 nm to 950 nm the up-converted emission peak changes from 415 nm to 516 nm. The strongest emission appears at 531 nm when the excitation wavelength is 850 nm which can be belong to the n→π* transition of C=N. Comparing with the normal PL spectrum, the peak of UCPL has a red-shift which is caused by the IR laser heating and self-absorption processes. Indeed, the similar red-shift of UCPL in near infrared region can also happen on the GQDs [24] and organic dyes [25].

From the above fluorescent behavior of the N-GQDs, a possible energy level structural model of the N-GQDs was established to study the normal PL and the up-converted PL properties. Fig. 5b is the schematic illustrations of two electronic transitions processes of N-GQDs. It is obviously that there are C and N states, which are related to the π* orbital of the C=N bonds in the N-GQDs. When the excitation energy is about 3.10 eV (400 nm), the light was absorbed by the chromophore containing the C=N group and the green light (2.32 eV, 535 nm) was emitted. For the O state, the irradiation of 3.64 eV (340 nm) excites the n→π* transition of C=O and causes the emission of 2.69 eV (461 nm). By contrast, when the excitation energy is about 1.46 eV (850 nm), one electron will absorb two photons and transit to the π* orbital of N due to the higher energy (2.92 eV). Then the green light (2.31 eV, 537 nm) is emitted for excitation electron backing to ground state.

With large scale absorption, N-GQDs could have a prospect of being employed as a sensitizer in photovoltaic conversion. Jeon et al. [26] reported the GQDs/TiO2 composites had an excellent photocatalytic activity due to the interaction between GQDs and TiO2 nano-particles.

By combining the N-GQDs and TiO2 into a composite system, the visible light region could be used efficiently. Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b shows the O 1s XPS spectra of the N-GQDs/TiO2 composites (Solid 1 and 2). Fig. 6a shows three peaks at 529.4 eV (Ti-O), 531.1 eV (C=O) and 532.3 eV (C-O-C/C-OH) [24,28]. The spectrum of Fig. 6b includes three peaks: 529.5 eV (Ti-O), 531.7 eV (Ti-O-N) and 533.1 eV [27,28]. Comparing two spectra, it would be found obviously that Fig. 6a has a higher peak of Ti-O-N bonds. It means there are more bonds generated in N-GQDs/TiO2 after the composites with isothermy process. In addition, the photocatalytic performances of two samples are discrepant. As Fig. 6c exhibits, the single precursor makes a poor performance in degrading rhodamine B (RhB). When the N-GQDs were added into RhB solution, RhB has about 10% degradation after irradiation (>400 nm) for 60 min, which is approximate to the self-degradation of RhB (about 8%). That implies that N-GQDs almost have no photocatalytic ability under visible light. The pure TiO2 (P25) has about 26% degradation of RhB, a higher photocatalytic ability than pure N-GQDs under visible light. But on the contrary, the degradation is more than 66% and 90% for the N-GQDs/TiO2 composites Solid 1 and Solid 2. These results indicate that the N-GQDs absorption in visible region is the reason to explain the enhanced photocatalytic activity of the composites. Furthermore, Fig. 6d also exhibits that two different synthetic methods cause the different properties. By the comparison of the O1s XPS spectra of Solid 1 and 2, it is marked that the N-GQDs/TiO2 composite has the higher degradation rate as well as a higher concentration of Ti-O-N bonds (531.7 eV) which connect the TiO2 and the N-GQDs. For this relation, a probable interpretation is: with the isothermy process, more Ti-O-N bonds are formed between TiO2 and the N-GQDs, and act as a path for the conduction of excitation electrons. When the N-GQDs/TiO2 under the irradiation of lamp, N-GQDs will get the photon energy and generate excitation electrons. As a kind of n-type semiconductors, TiO2 tend to promote charge separation after accepting electrons. This nature implies that the excitation electrons, which should have jumped to ground state and emitted fluorescence, are injected into the TiO2 by these bonds between the TiO2 and the N-GQDs.

In Fig. S4, The PL spectra (Fig. S4a) and the time resolved photoluminescence decay measurements (Fig. S4b, excitation at 410 nm) of the precursors and the composites can provide a direct comparison about their photoelectric properties. Both Solid 1 and 2 possess the similar PL decay time to the N-GQDs (N-GQDs: τ1 ≈ 3.91 ns, τ2 ≈ 8.23 ns; Solid 1: τ1 ≈ 3.30 ns, τ2 ≈ 8.48 ns; and Solid 2: τ1 ≈ 3.01 ns, τ2 ≈ 8.44 ns). And the PL spectra (Fig. S4a) exhibit the obviously photo-quenching on the N-GQDs/TiO2. For describing the RhB degradation with composites intuitively, a possible mechanism is illustrated in Scheme 2 . TiO2 nanocrystals obtain electron from N-GQDs (if there are some bonds between them, this process will be more efficient), cause separation of holes and electrons in TiO2. This charge separation process promotes the formation of active oxygen (·O2, on TiO2) and hydroxyl (·OH, on N-GQDs) radicals to oxidize the RhB molecules. For the ability to enlarge the absorption region and intensify the electrons transportation of the substrates, the N-GQDs can be applied as the new green dye sensitizer to integrating into many applications of organic degradation and photo-electric conversion.

In summary, we have developed a facile approach to produce a series of light-tunable N-GQDs via the isothermy reactions of various NCA/Nurea ratios at 140 °C for a certain period of time. The N-GQDs, with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 38% in ethanol solution, have two fluorescent centers which respond to the oxygen containing groups and nitrogen containing groups on as-prepared N-GQDs, respectively. On account of the broad, two-regions absorption, the N-GQDs/TiO2 composite showed more excellent photocatalytic performance than the pure TiO2, which indicates possible potentials in solar energy conversion and pollution degradation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Science Foundation of China (51402140, U1632129), the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (lzujbky-2016-128).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.04.032.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three colors emission from S, N Co-doped graphene quantum dots for visible light H2 production and bio-imaging

Hydrothermal route for cutting graphene sheets into blue-luminescent graphene quantum dots

Insights into few-layer epitaxial graphene growth on 4H-SiC(0001) substrates from STM studies
Facile synthesis and characterization of graphene nanosheets
